title: GridRoute: A Benchmark for LLM-Based Route Planning with Cardinal Movement in Grid Environments
authors: Kechen Li, Yaotian Tao, Ximing Wen, Quanwei Sun, Zifei Gong, Hongli Zhang, Chang Xu, Xizhe Zhang, Tianbo Ji
venue: arXiv v2 (originally submitted 30 May 2025; v2 revised 13 Aug 2025); cs.AI; not (yet) published at a peer-reviewed venue per arXiv listing; code at https://github.com/LinChance/GridRoute
claim: GridRoute is a benchmark that evaluates whether classical pathfinding algorithms (A*, Dijkstra, DFS) can be embedded into LLM prompts ("Algorithm of Planning", AoP) to improve route planning in N×N cardinal-movement grids with rectangular obstacles, compared against independent prompting (Vanilla, CoT, Few-shot). The central claim is that AoP prompting significantly outperforms Vanilla prompting and is comparable to or better than CoT, especially as map size/complexity grows, and that few-shot examples can further improve algorithm-guided prompting -- demonstrating complementary strengths of LLMs and classical algorithms for hybrid neuro-symbolic planning.
novelty: The authors claim two contributions: (1) GridRoute itself, a comprehensive benchmark comparing independent prompting vs. algorithm-guided strategies across six LLMs (7B-72B/671B) and three grid sizes (10x10, 20x20, 30x30), evaluated on six metrics (Compliance Ratio, Feasibility Ratio, Optimal Ratio, Geometric Mean, MSE, Runtime) plus a five-way failure taxonomy; and (2) 'Algorithm of Planning' (AoP), a novel hybrid prompting technique that embeds the reasoning mechanics of DFS/A*/Dijkstra directly into the prompt's instructions (not just naming the algorithm), which they distinguish from prior work like PPNL (CoT/ReAct only, no algorithm embedding) and LLM-A* (uses the LLM for only one sub-step -- generating intermediate targets -- while A* still produces the final solution). GridRoute's gap claim is that no prior benchmark systematically studies how much traditional algorithmic guidance (embedded fully in-prompt) helps LLM-generated route planning end-to-end.

protocol.description: Each test instance is one LLM call: the model is given the full grid size, all obstacle coordinates, and origin/destination coordinates in a single natural-language prompt, and must return a complete path (a list of coordinates) in one response. There is no multi-turn interaction, no incremental observation, and no feedback loop between LLM and environment/oracle within an instance -- the whole grid state and task is presented upfront and the full output path is generated in one forward pass (Sec 3.1 'Task Description'; Appendix A prompt templates).
protocol.observation_content: The model sees, in a single text prompt: (1) the full set of obstacle coordinates, given as a list of rectangles/corner-pairs, e.g. 'Obstacles: [((1, 2), (3, 4)), ((2, 5), (4, 6))]' (Appendix A.1, Table 3 FewShot-Base example); (2) the starting point (start_x, start_y) and ending point (end_x, end_y) as raw coordinates; (3) grid size is referenced in the templated instructions but the grid itself is NOT rendered as an ASCII/ visual grid -- it is described purely via obstacle-rectangle coordinates and start/end coordinates (no cell-by-cell grid drawing appears anywhere in the quoted prompt templates in Appendix A). For CoT and AoP prompts, the model additionally receives explicit step-by-step algorithmic instructions (e.g., 'Use A* algorithm...Use the Manhattan distance as the heuristic function...' Table 5) embedded directly in the same single prompt. For FewShot-Base/Algo-Direct/Algo-Reasoning, the same single prompt additionally includes 1-2 complete worked input-output examples (obstacles + start/end + full example path, and for Algo-Reasoning a fully worked step-by-step Dijkstra trace with running distance updates, Table 8). No intermediate state (partial path so far, feedback on validity, distance-to-wall) is fed back to the model mid-generation -- the entire output path is produced from the single upfront prompt (Sec 3.1; Appendix A.1-A.3).

representations.formats_compared: Only one input representation is used throughout: coordinate-list / rectangle-corner description of obstacles plus (x,y) start/end coordinates, verbalized into natural-language prompt templates (Sec 3.1 'Task Description': 'we design standardized templates to verbalize structured task information, including grid size, obstacle locations, and origin/destination coordinates, into consistent and interpretable natural language prompts'). No ASCII-grid rendering, adjacency-list, or visual/matrix representation is compared -- the paper varies PROMPTING STRATEGY (Vanilla, CoT, FewShot-Base, AoP-DFS, AoP-A*, AoP-Dijkstra, Algo-Direct, Algo-Reasoning) and MAP SIZE (10x10, 20x20, 30x30), not input modality/representation format.
representations.effect_on_accuracy: unverified — missing from extraction (no representation-format ablation is reported; the paper's independent variables are prompting strategy and grid size, not representation format)

models.name: DeepSeek-V3 (671B)
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. E.g. Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10: 48/47; CoT FR/OR: 73/72; Few-shot FR/OR: 85/84; AoP-A* FR/OR: 80/80 (GM=100, MSE=0); AoP-Dijkstra FR/OR: 81/79; Algo-Direct FR/OR: 84/82; Algo-Reasoning FR/OR: 82/79. Consistently one of the strongest and most stable performers across all prompt types and sizes.

models.name: Qwen2.5-7B-instruct
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10: 48/47, dropping to 13/13 at 30x30. AoP-Dijkstra FR/OR at 10x10: 57/55, dropping to 23/20 at 30x30 (RT spikes to 66.42s). Algo-Reasoning (Table 11) fails completely: CR/FR/OR = 0/0/0 (GM/MSE = NaN) at ALL three map sizes -- 'due to the limited capabilities of Qwen2.5-7B, Algo-Reasoning could not produce valid outputs on this smaller model' (Sec 5, RQ2).

models.name: Qwen2.5-32B-instruct
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10: 63/45; at 30x30: 64/61. AoP-Dijkstra FR/OR at 20x20 drops sharply: reported as 0.51/0.45 (likely a formatting/decimal artifact in the extracted table given ranges elsewhere are 0-100; flagged as an extraction anomaly). Algo-Reasoning FR/OR at 30x30: 39/0.3 (also anomalously low OR value, flagged). RQ3 notes scaling from 7B→32B gives 'substantial improvements in FR and RT.'

models.name: Qwen2.5-72B-instruct
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10: 72/69 (NOTE: this is NOT the best Vanilla FR at 10x10 -- ChatGPT-4's Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10 is 76/63, higher FR than Qwen2.5-72B's 72; corrected per verification pass). AoP-A* FR/OR at 10x10: 77/73. RQ3: 'The 72B model further enhances FR while significantly boosting OR and reducing GM and MSE... Notably, Qwen2.5-72B achieves performance comparable to models such as GPT-4 Turbo(1 trillion) and DeepSeek-V3(671B), suggesting that simply increasing model size in GridRoute tasks eventually encounters a scaling law bottleneck' (Sec 5, RQ3).

models.name: LLaMA3.1-70B-instruct
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. AoP-A* is its strongest condition: FR/OR at 10x10: 82/81 (GM=100.30, MSE=0.05), at 20x20: 74/73, at 30x30: 70/67 -- among the best OR scores in the whole study.

models.name: GPT-4 Turbo (ChatGPT-4, ~1T)
models.key_results: Table 9/10/11. CoT FR/OR at 10x10: 84/77 (best CoT result in the table). AoP-DFS collapses catastrophically: CR/FR/OR = 3/3/3 at 10x10, 1/1/1 at 20x20 and 30x30 (Table 10) -- the single most extreme failure in the results tables, implying GPT-4 Turbo almost never produces a compliant/feasible route under the AoP-DFS prompt.

headline_numbers: AoP prompting methods significantly outperform Vanilla across all metrics except AoP-DFS, especially in more complex environments (Sec 5, RQ1, referencing Figure 3).

AoP achieves comparable or even superior performance to CoT, particularly on Qwen2.5-7B (Sec 5, RQ1, referencing Figure 12); but CoT is more consistent across all models with fewer failure cases overall (Sec 5, RQ1, referencing Figure 2).
FewShot-Base achieves performance comparable to AoP-Dijkstra despite its simplicity (Sec 5, RQ1).

Algo-Direct (simple worked example) generally enhances AoP-Dijkstra performance in most metrics; Algo-Reasoning (detailed worked trace) improves GM and MSE specifically but AT THE COST of reduced FR and OR, and fails to produce any valid output at all on Qwen2.5-7B (CR/FR/OR = 0/0/0, Table 11) (Sec 5, RQ2).
Algo-Direct outperforms FewShot-Base when using the same worked example, by embedding it in the AoP-Dijkstra framework rather than a plain few-shot prompt (Sec 5, RQ2).
Qwen2.5-72B performance is comparable to GPT-4 Turbo (~1T params) and DeepSeek-V3 (671B) on GridRoute tasks, implying a scaling-law bottleneck for pure model-size increases on this task (Sec 5, RQ3).

Map size (10→20→30) is the dominant driver of performance decline: FR and OR systematically decline and Run Time increases as map size grows, regardless of model size; the influence of model size shrinks as maps get larger (e.g. Qwen2.5-32B ≈ Qwen2.5-72B at 30x30) (Sec 5, RQ4, referencing Figure 6).
ChatGPT-4 (GPT-4 Turbo) under AoP-DFS collapses to CR/FR/OR = 3/3/3 (10x10), 1/1/1 (20x20 and 30x30) -- the most severe failure mode recorded in Table 10.

failure_modes.taxonomy: Sec 3.3 defines five failure types: (1) Invalid Step Distance -- non-unit-length/diagonal moves violating grid movement rules; (2) Path Through Obstacle -- path intersects an obstacle region; (3) Out of Bounds -- path exceeds map boundaries, 'revealing the LLMs' lack of spatial awareness'; (4) Empty Path -- LLM produces no path output; (5) Start/End Mismatch -- path deviates from specified origin or fails to reach destination.
failure_modes.trends: As model size increases (Qwen2.5 7B->32B->72B), illegal moves and start/end mismatches become significantly less frequent and errors increasingly concentrate in path-through-obstacle cases (Sec 5, RQ3). As map size increases, the dominant error type shifts from primarily 'Path through obstacles' at small sizes to 'more intricate types of errors' at larger sizes (Sec 5, RQ4). AoP exhibits significant start/end mismatch issues specifically in smaller models (e.g. Qwen2.5-7B) (Sec 5, RQ1). AoP-DFS shows a notable trade-off: despite improving FR/OR over Vanilla, its GM and MSE can sometimes be worse (higher error), and it shows less consistent runtime scaling with model size, even showing increased latency from 7B to 32B (Sec 5, RQ1 and RQ3). Path-through-obstacle errors persist as a stubborn residual failure mode even with larger models and better prompting, flagged as a limitation requiring mechanisms like 'obstacle collision backtracking or targeted fine-tuning' (Sec 7, Limitations).

one_shot_vs_multistep: GridRoute is entirely single-shot/single-forward-pass at the protocol level: every experimental condition (Vanilla, CoT, FewShot-Base, AoP-DFS/A*/Dijkstra, Algo-Direct, Algo-Reasoning) asks the LLM to produce the ENTIRE path in one response given the entire problem statement upfront -- there is no multi-turn/interactive loop with environment feedback anywhere in the described protocol (Sec 3.1, Appendix A). The paper's one-shot vs multi-step distinction is therefore NOT about single-call vs. interactive-loop prediction (as in c19's design); it is about whether the single call is asked to show step-by-step algorithmic/CoT REASONING internally before emitting the final path (CoT, AoP prompts) versus emitting the path directly (Vanilla). Within this single-shot family, more explicit internal step-by-step reasoning (CoT, AoP) generally improves feasibility/optimality over direct Vanilla generation, especially as environments get larger/more complex (Sec 5, RQ1), suggesting models benefit from being prompted to internally simulate multi-step algorithmic procedures even though the interaction itself remains a single forward generation of the full output.

relation_to_candidate.quadrant: Full-observation x Single-shot. GridRoute presents the complete grid problem (all obstacles, full grid size, start, end) in one prompt and elicits the complete output path in a single forward generation -- there is no partial observability and no interactive/multi-turn loop with environment feedback.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c19: Both are deterministic, cardinal-direction (four-directional up/down/left/right) grid-movement tasks with programmatically verifiable ground truth (GridRoute uses a constrained diagonal Dijkstra oracle for shortest paths, Sec 3.1; c19 uses an independent object-model/simulator oracle). Both are single-shot in the sense that one LLM call produces the full answer without interactive feedback. Both study how much internal step-by-step algorithmic scaffolding in the prompt (GridRoute's CoT/AoP; c19's could analogously test explicit rule statements) affects accuracy on grid tasks, and both report that naive/vanilla prompting is not floor-level -- models get meaningful non-trivial performance even without algorithmic guidance (GridRoute Vanilla FR up to 76 at 10x10 for ChatGPT-4, Table 9), echoing c19's own stated concern that grid navigation is a familiar-enough skill that naive prompts may not floor low (c19.md, Round-1 rubric fit, 'Against').
relation_to_candidate.differences: GridRoute's output target is a full multi-step PATH (a list of coordinates), which is exactly the many-valid-answers ambiguity c19 is explicitly designed to avoid by asking for exactly ONE derived fact (final coordinate/heading/carrying-flag) scored by strict exact match (c19.md, 'What it proposes'). GridRoute's grid representation is NEVER an ASCII-rendered grid in the quoted prompts -- it is a coordinate/rectangle-obstacle description with no visual grid layout at all, whereas c19's protocol explicitly renders a full ASCII grid to the model. GridRoute has no invented/nonstandard commands, no wrap-vs-clamp boundary convention variation, and no heading/facing-direction or carrying-flag state -- its state space is purely (x,y) position with static rectangular obstacles, movement is unconstrained beyond obstacle-avoidance (no jump/wrap mechanics). GridRoute's grids are much larger (10x10, 20x20, 30x30) than c19's roughly-six-by-six design. GridRoute's few-shot ablation (Algo-Direct vs Algo-Reasoning) shows a genuine ACCURACY TRADE-OFF (detailed worked-trace few-shot improves GM/MSE but hurts FR/OR, and breaks entirely on a 7B model) rather than a clean uniform improvement.
relation_to_candidate.what_c19_would_add: c19 would add: (1) full ASCII grid rendering as the observation (GridRoute never renders a grid visually); (2) a single-derived-fact target that eliminates path-ambiguity scoring problems inherent to GridRoute's path-based Feasibility/Optimal-Ratio metrics; (3) nonstandard/invented command semantics (jump, wrap) layered on top of standard cardinal movement, which GridRoute does not test at all; (4) a much smaller grid scale (~6x6) intended to keep the task tractable for exact-match single-token scoring rather than GridRoute's 10-30 size sweep aimed at measuring path-planning degradation; (5) heading/orientation and carrying-flag state tracking, which GridRoute's pure (x,y)-coordinate-and-obstacle-avoidance formulation does not include.

corpus_claim: GridRoute is a cardinal-movement grid route-planning benchmark; c19 flips its plan-generation into single-token state prediction. It benchmarks classical-algorithm-guided prompting (A*, Dijkstra, DFS embedded in the prompt), and few-shot demos of worked traces measurably help (shown in GridRoute's Algo-Reasoning).
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The first two clauses are supported: GridRoute is indeed a cardinal-movement (4-directional up/down/left/right) grid route-planning benchmark (Sec 3.1), and it does benchmark classical-algorithm-guided prompting with DFS, A*, and Dijkstra embedded directly into the prompt instructions via the 'Algorithm of Planning' (AoP) framework (Sec 4.2.2, Appendix A.2, Tables 4-6). However, the final clause -- 'few-shot demos of worked traces measurably help (shown in GridRoute's Algo-Reasoning)' -- is an overstatement/mischaracterization of the paper's actual finding. The paper reports a TRADE-OFF, not unambiguous help: Algo-Reasoning (the detailed worked-trace few-shot variant) improves GM and MSE specifically but 'at the cost of reduced FR and OR' compared to Algo-Direct (Sec 5, RQ2), and it fails catastrophically on the smallest model (Qwen2.5-7B produces 0/0/0 CR/FR/OR at all three map sizes, Table 11) -- the paper explicitly attributes this to 'the limited capabilities of Qwen2.5-7B.' Moreover, the paper's own comparison shows the SIMPLER worked example (Algo-Direct) 'generally enhances AoP-Dijkstra performance in most metrics' more broadly/consistently than the complex worked-trace (Algo-Reasoning). So 'worked traces measurably help' is true only for two of six metrics (GM, MSE) and only on models capable of using them; it is false or catastrophically negative for FR/OR and for the smallest model tested. The claim as written should be corrected to: worked-trace few-shot examples (Algo-Reasoning) trade improved route-similarity-to-optimal (GM/MSE) for reduced feasibility/optimality rates (FR/OR) versus a simpler worked-example variant (Algo-Direct), and can fail entirely on smaller models.
baseline_relevant: Grid sizes used: three configurations of increasing complexity -- {N=10, n=2 obstacles, s=3 obstacle size}, {N=20, n=3, s=4}, {N=30, n=4, s=5}; 5 origin-destination pairs sampled per environment with minimum Euclidean distance >= 30% of the grid diagonal, connectivity verified via 4-directional BFS (Sec 3.1). This is much larger than c19's proposed ~6x6 grids.

Ground-truth oracle: a constrained diagonal Dijkstra algorithm computes reference shortest paths and optimal path lengths for every test instance (Sec 3.1) -- analogous in spirit to c19's independent simulator oracle, though GridRoute's oracle produces a full path rather than a single derived fact.

Prompt format effects: NO ASCII-grid rendering is used anywhere -- all conditions describe the grid purely via rectangular obstacle corner-coordinates and start/end (x,y) coordinates embedded in natural-language templates (Appendix A). This means GridRoute offers no evidence on ASCII-grid-vs-coordinate-list representation effects for our baseline design; that comparison is unverified from this paper and would need to come from other corpus papers (e.g., the c19.md-cited MazeEval or the 2026 spatial-world-models maze study).

Metrics used: Compliance Ratio (CR, format-valid output), Feasibility Ratio (FR, valid + obstacle-free), Optimal Ratio (OR, matches Dijkstra-optimal length), Geometric Mean (GM, ratio of generated to optimal path length, 1.0 = perfect), Mean Square Error (MSE, squared length deviation from optimal, 0 = perfect), Run Time in seconds (Sec 3.2).

Floors/ceilings observed: Vanilla-prompt Feasibility Ratio ranges from as low as 11 (DeepSeek-V3 at 30x30, Table 9) up to 76-85 (ChatGPT-4/DeepSeek-V3 at 10x10 under few-shot/CoT, Tables 9); AoP-DFS is a notable ceiling-breaker in the WRONG direction for GPT-4 Turbo, collapsing to CR/FR/OR = 1-3 out of 100 (Table 10) -- demonstrating that algorithm-in-prompt framing can catastrophically backfire on some models rather than uniformly help.

Contamination/data-release status: code is open-sourced at https://github.com/LinChance/GridRoute (Abstract); grids/obstacles/origin-destination pairs are randomly generated per the described procedure (Sec 3.1), suggesting the underlying task instances are regenerable/contamination-resistant in the same spirit c19 aims for, though the paper does not explicitly discuss contamination risk for its own released benchmark data.

Model set and scale: six LLMs from 7B to 72B/671B/~1T parameters -- Qwen2.5 (7B, 32B, 72B), LLaMA3.1-70B, DeepSeek-V3 (671B), GPT-4 Turbo (~1T) (Sec 4.1) -- useful reference points for what parameter scales were tested on comparable grid-navigation-style tasks.
quotes: "To fill this gap, we propose a comprehensive evaluation benchmark GridRoute to assess how LLMs can take advantage of traditional algorithms. We also propose a novel hybrid prompting technique called Algorithm of Planning (AoP), which introduces traditional algorithms' guidance into prompting." (Abstract)

"In GridRoute, LLMs are required to discover a valid path from the origin O to the destination D in a randomly generated N×N grid environment, while avoiding a set of n non-overlapping square obstacles of size s×s. The movement of the agent is constrained to four cardinal directions: up, down, left, and right." (Sec 3.1, Data Generation)

"For evaluation, we employ a constrained diagonal Dijkstra algorithm to compute the shortest paths for all task instances, yielding both the reference trajectory and the corresponding optimal path length." (Sec 3.1, Task Description)

"AoP prompting methods significantly outperform the Vanilla prompt across all metrics except AoP-DFS, especially in more complex environments... a notable trade-off exists for AoP-DFS compared to Vanilla: despite the improvements in FR and OR, GM and MSE can sometimes be higher." (Sec 5, RQ1)

"Our experiments comparing AoP-Dijkstra with different shot strategies (Figure 5) reveal that the Algo-Direct prompt generally enhances AoP-Dijkstra performance in most metrics, while the Algo-Reasoning setting improves its performance specifically in GM and MSE at the cost of reduced FR and OR. However, due to the limited capabilities of Qwen2.5-7B, Algo-Reasoning could not produce valid outputs on this smaller model." (Sec 5, RQ2)

"Notably, Qwen2.5-72B achieves performance comparable to models such as GPT-4 Turbo(1 trillion) and DeepSeek-V3(671B), suggesting that simply increasing model size in GridRoute tasks eventually encounters a scaling law bottleneck." (Sec 5, RQ3)
"As the map size increases, both the FR and OR exhibit a systematic decline, while the RT correspondingly increases. This scaling effect holds consistently regardless of model size..." (Sec 5, RQ4)
"Out of Bounds: Reflects paths exceeding map boundaries, revealing the LLMs' lack of spatial awareness regarding environmental constraints." (Sec 3.3, Route Failure Types)

corrections: models[Qwen2.5-72B-instruct].key_results: dossier claimed Qwen2.5-72B's Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10 (72/69) was 'best among Vanilla/independent prompts at that size.' This is false: ChatGPT-4's Vanilla FR/OR at 10x10 is 76/63 (Table 9, row 'ChatGPT-4 | Vanilla'), which has a higher FR (76 > 72) than Qwen2.5-72B. Corrected the key_results text to note this and cite the actual ChatGPT-4 figures.
notes_no_change_needed: protocol.mode = single-shot verified correct for the entire paper: GridRoute has no interactive/multi-turn track to flatten. The only mentions of ReAct/interactive-style prompting are citations of *other* papers (PPNL) in Sec 1/2, not part of GridRoute's own protocol; all GridRoute prompt conditions (Vanilla, CoT, FewShot-Base, AoP-DFS/A*/Dijkstra, Algo-Direct, Algo-Reasoning) are single LLM calls producing the full path in one response (Appendix A).

observation_content checked against Appendix A.1-A.3 prompt templates verbatim: confirmed no ASCII/visual grid rendering anywhere; obstacles given only as rectangle corner-coordinate pairs plus (x,y) start/end.

All per-model numeric claims (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen2.5-32B, LLaMA3.1-70B, ChatGPT-4/GPT-4 Turbo) checked cell-by-cell against Tables 9/10/11 in the source markdown; all other reported CR/FR/OR/GM/MSE/RT figures matched exactly, including the anomalous Qwen2.5-32B AoP-Dijkstra 20x20 FR/OR = 0.51/0.45 and Algo-Reasoning 30x30 OR = 0.3, both of which are genuine artifacts present in the source paper's own table (not dossier extraction errors) and were already appropriately flagged as anomalies by the dossier.
All 8 verbatim quotes located and confirmed exact-match in source (Abstract; Sec 3.1 Data Generation and Task Description; Sec 5 RQ1, RQ2, RQ3, RQ4; Sec 3.3 Route Failure Types).
Five-way failure taxonomy (Invalid Step Distance, Path Through Obstacle, Out of Bounds, Empty Path, Start/End Mismatch) matches Sec 3.3 verbatim.
Environment settings ({N=10,n=2,s=3}, {N=20,n=3,s=4}, {N=30,n=4,s=5}, 5 O-D pairs, 30% diagonal min distance, 4-directional BFS connectivity check) match Sec 3.1 Environment Setting verbatim.
corpus_claim_verdict's characterization of Algo-Reasoning as a trade-off (helps GM/MSE, hurts FR/OR, fails on Qwen2.5-7B) is correct per Sec 5 RQ2 and Table 11.
